issues.description: FR-1b spec deviation: round 1 always skips the judge unconditionally (line 461: round <= 1 returns None) even when verdict.clean==false. The spec says verdict.clean==false alone is a trigger. This means a round-1 verdict with only low-severity cosmetic nits will NOT get triviality override (Problem 1). The code comment acknowledges this, but the deviation is undocumented in any operator-facing config or spec amendment. With default judge_min_round=2, round 2 IS covered, but round 1 is a hard skip regardless of configuration.
issues.suggestion: This is an acceptable tradeoff but should be documented in the OrchestratorConfig doc comment or nemo.toml defaults. Consider noting that judge_min_round cannot override the round <= 1 skip.

issues.description: FR-1c says the judge is NOT invoked on 'clean==true AND round==1', implying round 2+ clean verdicts after prior non-clean verdicts were potentially intended to pass through the judge. The code skips the judge for ALL clean verdicts at every round. While the behavior is reasonable (clean verdicts are unambiguous), it is a literal spec deviation from FR-1c's targeted exemption. The code comment documents this as intentional, which is good.
issues.suggestion: No code change needed — the behavior is sound and documented. Track as a spec clarification item.

issues.description: No integration test verifies the harden audit-found-issues path with Continue at max_rounds. The harden audit evaluation path (driver.rs ~line 688-692) has a max_rounds guard that fails instead of dispatching a doomed revise. This specific code path (judge Continue + harden audit + max_rounds) is not exercised by any test. The existing test_harden_revise_judge_continue_at_max_rounds_fails covers the revise-evaluation path at max_rounds, but not the audit-evaluation path.
issues.suggestion: Add an integration test that sets up a hardening loop at max_rounds with a completed audit job (not revise), has the judge return Continue, and verifies FAILED state with the 'Judge wanted to continue' reasoning.

issues.description: Token budget math is tight but workable with the current constants (MAX_SPEC_CHARS=4K, MAX_ROUND_CHARS=3K). However, the progressive truncation guard at MAX_INPUT_CHARS=24K (~6K tokens) is relatively close to the 8K token budget from FR-4b when accounting for the system prompt sent separately (~2K chars). The gap between field budgets (4K+3K+2K=9K chars ~2.25K tokens) and the progressive truncation trigger (24K chars ~6K tokens) means inputs between 9K and 24K chars undergo no truncation at all, relying solely on the per-field limits. This is fine if per-field limits hold, but recurring_findings (capped at 10 entries) with verbose entries could push into that gap.
issues.suggestion: Consider adding a per-entry character limit on recurring findings descriptions (e.g., 200 chars each), or reducing MAX_INPUT_CHARS to 20K for more conservative headroom.

issues.description: evaluate() takes 9 parameters with #[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]. While functional and called from one place (invoke_judge_for_stage), this makes the API fragile. If Stage 2 adds more callers or parameters, it will be harder to maintain.
issues.suggestion: Consider bundling parameters into a JudgeEvaluateContext struct if the method gains more callers in Stage 2. Low priority for Stage 1.

issues.description: The orphaned judge decisions summary line uses a count threshold but doesn't display the actual orphaned decisions. If a judge decision has a round number that doesn't match any RoundRecord (edge case from timing), the user only sees 'N additional judge decision(s) not shown inline' with no way to see them except raw JSON. The suggestion to 'use --json for details' references a flag that doesn't appear to be implemented.
issues.suggestion: Either implement a --json flag for the inspect command, or change the message to something like 'use API endpoint for details'.

issues.description: The current_verdict truncation block extracts the description as a String via a complex conditional, then compares its length and truncates. The conditional chain `if let Some(desc) = issue.get_mut("description").and_then(|d| d.as_str().map(|s| s.to_string())) && desc.len() > MAX_ISSUE_DESC_CHARS` creates an owned String even when no truncation is needed, which is a minor inefficiency.
issues.suggestion: Check length before allocating: use as_str() first to check length, only allocate when truncation is actually needed.
